title: Pemphigus Vulgaris
oneLiner: Autoimmune blistering disease causing flaccid blisters and painful mucosal erosions; life-threatening if widespread due to infection and fluid loss.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN):** widespread target lesions, fever, prodrome, positive Nikolsky sign but also tense blisters—urgent IVIG/cyclosporine, stop culprit drug, not steroids.  
  *Single finding: fever + target lesions + recent drug exposure.*
- **Sepsis from denuded skin:** if >10% BSA involved, risk of rapid bacterial superinfection.  
  *Single finding: spreading erythema beyond blisters, tachycardia, rising lactate.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painful, non-healing mouth ulcers (first sign in 50–70%)
  - Flaccid, easily ruptured blisters on skin (trunk, scalp, flexures)
  - Positive Nikolsky sign (gentle lateral pressure on intact skin causes blister extension)
  - Painful genital, nasal, or ocular mucosal erosions
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Blister roof easily rubbed off leaving raw, painful base
  - No “target” lesions or tense blisters (unlike pemphigoid)
  - Extensive exudative erosions without preceding blister (if ruptured)
  - Examine all mucosa: oral, conjunctival, genital, anal
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely:
  1. **Bullous pemphigoid** — tense blisters on flexures, often pruritic, no mucosal involvement (or mild), negative Nikolsky.
  2. **Dermatitis herpetiformis** — intensely pruritic herpetiform vesicles on elbows/knees/buttocks, gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
  3. **Linear IgA disease** — annular blisters (“cluster of jewels”), mucosal involvement possible, IgA deposition on immunofluorescence.
  4. **Erythema multiforme** — target lesions, acral distribution, ± mucosal, usually drug/infection-related.
  5. **Mucous membrane pemphigoid** — scarring of conjunctiva, oral/genital ulcers, tense blisters heal with scarring.
- **Splitter:** Positive Nikolsky sign + painful mucosal erosions → strongly favour Pemphigus Vulgaris over Bullous Pemphigoid.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Nikolsky sign (simple bedside test)
  - Oral examination with good light; swab for MC&S if infected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, LFT (baseline before immunosuppression)
  - Anti-desmoglein 1 & 3 ELISA (high sensitivity/specificity for diagnosis and severity)
  - Varicella serology, HIV, hepatitis B/C screening (before immunosuppression)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (if suspecting TB or atypical infection before steroids)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch biopsy of fresh blister (perilesional) for H&E** — shows acantholysis (intraepidermal suprabasal split)
  - **Direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin** — IgG and C3 deposition intercellularly (“chicken wire” pattern)
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for H&E and direct immunofluorescence** – omitting this delays definitive diagnosis and risks inappropriate treatment (e.g. steroids for bullous pemphigoid).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess BSA of blistering/erosions; calculate fluid loss (if >10%, consider burns-like fluid replacement – [check local policy])
  - Pain relief: oral morphine sulfate 5–10 mg PRN if severe oral pain
  - Start IV fluids if unable to drink, oral care with chlorhexidine mouthwash
  - Arrange urgent dermatology consultation
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **High-dose oral prednisolone** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (start 40–60 mg daily) – is first-line
  - If severe (rapid spread, >30% BSA, refractory): IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days (under specialist guidance)
  - **Steroid-sparing agent:** add azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day or mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg–1 g BD once stable (check TPMT before azathioprine)
  - **Rituximab** (increasingly first-line in moderate-severe disease): 1 g IV on day 0 and day 14 (after infection screen)
  - Topical: emollients (50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin), non-adherent dressings on erosions
  - Oral care: benzydamine spray, nystatin suspension for oral Candida prophylaxis
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Taper prednisolone weekly by 5 mg once new blister formation stops (target 10–15 mg by 3–6 months)
  - Continue steroid-sparer for ≥12 months; monitor for relapse
  - Rituximab maintenance if needed (every 6 months)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline if oral intake poor; adjust for BSA loss
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 20–40 mg SC OD (provided no anticoagulant contraindication)
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral erosions; high protein supplements if catabolic
  - **Observations:** Baseline NEWS2; monitor temperature for sepsis; daily wound inspection
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Prednisolone: 40–60 mg PO OD (weight-based: 0.5–1 mg/kg); taper after disease control
  - Rituximab: 1 g IV day 0 and day 14 (given by dermatology/oncology day unit)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azathioprine: 1–2 mg/kg PO OD (check TPMT; risk of myelosuppression)
  - Mycophenolate mofetil: 500 mg–1 g PO BD (less hepatotoxic than azathioprine)
  - IV methylprednisolone: 500 mg–1 g IV OD for 3 days (pulse therapy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid azathioprine in TPMT deficiency (risk of pancytopenia)
  - Rituximab: contraindicated if active severe infection; screen for hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB
  - Prednisolone: avoid in uncontrolled systemic fungal infections; give with PPI and calcium/vitamin D
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in moderate–severe hepatic impairment; monitor LFTs
  - Mycophenolate: no dose adjustment needed; caution in GFR <25 mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prednisolone – starting too high (≥1.5 mg/kg) in elderly** → increased risk of psychosis, hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, and infection; always use lowest effective dose. **Rituximab – give without infection screen** → risk of fatal hepatitis B reactivation.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis from secondary infection of denuded skin (Staph, Strep)
  - Hypovolaemic shock if large BSA loss (rare in isolated PV)
  - Corticosteroid-induced hyperglycaemia, psychosis, peptic ulcer
- **Chronic:**
  - Relapse (common, requires long-term immunosuppression)
  - Oral/oesophageal scarring leading to dysphagia
  - Osteoporosis, Cushing’s syndrome, cataracts (from steroids)
  - Increased risk of skin cancers (squamous cell carcinoma) in long-term immunosuppression
sections.Prognosis:
- With modern treatment (steroids + rituximab or steroid-sparer): mortality <10% (previously 75% without steroids).
- Chronic relapsing course; ~50% achieve long-term remission off therapy within 5 years.
- Poor prognosis: extensive mucosal disease, older age, high titre anti-desmoglein 3 antibodies.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine all mucosal surfaces** – oral, genital, conjunctival, anal – to gauge severity and guide treatment escalation.
- **Start PPI and calcium/vitamin D with prednisolone** to prevent gastritis and osteoporosis.
- **Request urgent dermatology review and skin biopsy** before starting high-dose steroids if diagnosis uncertain (but treat empirically if strongly suspected).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Delaying biopsy** – missing the diagnosis leads to wrong treatment (e.g. steroids for pemphigoid vs. PV).
- **Using topical steroids alone** for mucosal lesions without systemic therapy – they will not control underlying autoimmunity.
- **Tapering steroids too quickly** – disease flares very common; taper by no more than 5 mg per week.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or spreading blisters/erosions, especially if covering >10% body
  - Fever, chills, redness around erosions (signs of infection)
  - Difficulty swallowing, drooling, or stridor (oesophageal/airway involvement)
  - Worsening pain or inability to tolerate oral fluids
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology clinic within 2–4 weeks; earlier if unstable
  - Monitor bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT) weekly – especially if on azathioprine or mycophenolate
  - Liaise with GP for steroid monitoring (glucose, bone density)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / dermatology registrar if:**
  - Rapid progression of skin involvement to >30% BSA within 24 h
  - Difficulty swallowing or breathing (suspect oesophageal/pharyngeal involvement)
  - Signs of sepsis: NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, new confusion
  - Failure to control disease after 5 days of high-dose prednisolone
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single parameter ≥3
  - Stridor, drooling, or stridor (airway compromise)
  - Severe hypoxia or hypotension unresponsive to fluid challenge

rawMarkdown:
## Pemphigus Vulgaris

**One-liner:** Autoimmune blistering disease causing flaccid blisters and painful mucosal erosions; life-threatening if widespread due to infection and fluid loss.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN):** widespread target lesions, fever, prodrome, positive Nikolsky sign but also tense blisters—urgent IVIG/cyclosporine, stop culprit drug, not steroids.  
  *Single finding: fever + target lesions + recent drug exposure.*
- **Sepsis from denuded skin:** if >10% BSA involved, risk of rapid bacterial superinfection.  
  *Single finding: spreading erythema beyond blisters, tachycardia, rising lactate.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painful, non-healing mouth ulcers (first sign in 50–70%)
  - Flaccid, easily ruptured blisters on skin (trunk, scalp, flexures)
  - Positive Nikolsky sign (gentle lateral pressure on intact skin causes blister extension)
  - Painful genital, nasal, or ocular mucosal erosions
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Blister roof easily rubbed off leaving raw, painful base
  - No “target” lesions or tense blisters (unlike pemphigoid)
  - Extensive exudative erosions without preceding blister (if ruptured)
  - Examine all mucosa: oral, conjunctival, genital, anal

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely:
  1. **Bullous pemphigoid** — tense blisters on flexures, often pruritic, no mucosal involvement (or mild), negative Nikolsky.
  2. **Dermatitis herpetiformis** — intensely pruritic herpetiform vesicles on elbows/knees/buttocks, gluten-sensitive enteropathy.
  3. **Linear IgA disease** — annular blisters (“cluster of jewels”), mucosal involvement possible, IgA deposition on immunofluorescence.
  4. **Erythema multiforme** — target lesions, acral distribution, ± mucosal, usually drug/infection-related.
  5. **Mucous membrane pemphigoid** — scarring of conjunctiva, oral/genital ulcers, tense blisters heal with scarring.
- **Splitter:** Positive Nikolsky sign + painful mucosal erosions → strongly favour Pemphigus Vulgaris over Bullous Pemphigoid.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Nikolsky sign (simple bedside test)
  - Oral examination with good light; swab for MC&S if infected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, LFT (baseline before immunosuppression)
  - Anti-desmoglein 1 & 3 ELISA (high sensitivity/specificity for diagnosis and severity)
  - Varicella serology, HIV, hepatitis B/C screening (before immunosuppression)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (if suspecting TB or atypical infection before steroids)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Punch biopsy of fresh blister (perilesional) for H&E** — shows acantholysis (intraepidermal suprabasal split)
  - **Direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin** — IgG and C3 deposition intercellularly (“chicken wire” pattern)
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy for H&E and direct immunofluorescence** – omitting this delays definitive diagnosis and risks inappropriate treatment (e.g. steroids for bullous pemphigoid).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess BSA of blistering/erosions; calculate fluid loss (if >10%, consider burns-like fluid replacement – [check local policy])
  - Pain relief: oral morphine sulfate 5–10 mg PRN if severe oral pain
  - Start IV fluids if unable to drink, oral care with chlorhexidine mouthwash
  - Arrange urgent dermatology consultation
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **High-dose oral prednisolone** 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (start 40–60 mg daily) – is first-line
  - If severe (rapid spread, >30% BSA, refractory): IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days (under specialist guidance)
  - **Steroid-sparing agent:** add azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day or mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg–1 g BD once stable (check TPMT before azathioprine)
  - **Rituximab** (increasingly first-line in moderate-severe disease): 1 g IV on day 0 and day 14 (after infection screen)
  - Topical: emollients (50:50 white soft paraffin/liquid paraffin), non-adherent dressings on erosions
  - Oral care: benzydamine spray, nystatin suspension for oral Candida prophylaxis
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Taper prednisolone weekly by 5 mg once new blister formation stops (target 10–15 mg by 3–6 months)
  - Continue steroid-sparer for ≥12 months; monitor for relapse
  - Rituximab maintenance if needed (every 6 months)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline if oral intake poor; adjust for BSA loss
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 20–40 mg SC OD (provided no anticoagulant contraindication)
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral erosions; high protein supplements if catabolic
  - **Observations:** Baseline NEWS2; monitor temperature for sepsis; daily wound inspection

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Prednisolone: 40–60 mg PO OD (weight-based: 0.5–1 mg/kg); taper after disease control
  - Rituximab: 1 g IV day 0 and day 14 (given by dermatology/oncology day unit)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azathioprine: 1–2 mg/kg PO OD (check TPMT; risk of myelosuppression)
  - Mycophenolate mofetil: 500 mg–1 g PO BD (less hepatotoxic than azathioprine)
  - IV methylprednisolone: 500 mg–1 g IV OD for 3 days (pulse therapy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid azathioprine in TPMT deficiency (risk of pancytopenia)
  - Rituximab: contraindicated if active severe infection; screen for hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB
  - Prednisolone: avoid in uncontrolled systemic fungal infections; give with PPI and calcium/vitamin D
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in moderate–severe hepatic impairment; monitor LFTs
  - Mycophenolate: no dose adjustment needed; caution in GFR <25 mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prednisolone – starting too high (≥1.5 mg/kg) in elderly** → increased risk of psychosis, hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, and infection; always use lowest effective dose. **Rituximab – give without infection screen** → risk of fatal hepatitis B reactivation.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis from secondary infection of denuded skin (Staph, Strep)
  - Hypovolaemic shock if large BSA loss (rare in isolated PV)
  - Corticosteroid-induced hyperglycaemia, psychosis, peptic ulcer
- **Chronic:**
  - Relapse (common, requires long-term immunosuppression)
  - Oral/oesophageal scarring leading to dysphagia
  - Osteoporosis, Cushing’s syndrome, cataracts (from steroids)
  - Increased risk of skin cancers (squamous cell carcinoma) in long-term immunosuppression

### Prognosis
- With modern treatment (steroids + rituximab or steroid-sparer): mortality <10% (previously 75% without steroids).
- Chronic relapsing course; ~50% achieve long-term remission off therapy within 5 years.
- Poor prognosis: extensive mucosal disease, older age, high titre anti-desmoglein 3 antibodies.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always examine all mucosal surfaces** – oral, genital, conjunctival, anal – to gauge severity and guide treatment escalation.
- **Start PPI and calcium/vitamin D with prednisolone** to prevent gastritis and osteoporosis.
- **Request urgent dermatology review and skin biopsy** before starting high-dose steroids if diagnosis uncertain (but treat empirically if strongly suspected).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Delaying biopsy** – missing the diagnosis leads to wrong treatment (e.g. steroids for pemphigoid vs. PV).
- **Using topical steroids alone** for mucosal lesions without systemic therapy – they will not control underlying autoimmunity.
- **Tapering steroids too quickly** – disease flares very common; taper by no more than 5 mg per week.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or spreading blisters/erosions, especially if covering >10% body
  - Fever, chills, redness around erosions (signs of infection)
  - Difficulty swallowing, drooling, or stridor (oesophageal/airway involvement)
  - Worsening pain or inability to tolerate oral fluids
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology clinic within 2–4 weeks; earlier if unstable
  - Monitor bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT) weekly – especially if on azathioprine or mycophenolate
  - Liaise with GP for steroid monitoring (glucose, bone density)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / dermatology registrar if:**
  - Rapid progression of skin involvement to >30% BSA within 24 h
  - Difficulty swallowing or breathing (suspect oesophageal/pharyngeal involvement)
  - Signs of sepsis: NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, new confusion
  - Failure to control disease after 5 days of high-dose prednisolone
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single parameter ≥3
  - Stridor, drooling, or stridor (airway compromise)
  - Severe hypoxia or hypotension unresponsive to fluid challenge
